notes.section_title: Introduction: Understanding Work in Our Lives

topics.topic_name: Work Around Us — A Story-Based Introduction

content.text: The chapter begins with a story about Anu and Kabir, who observe various people engaged in different types of work in their neighbourhood. Geeta Aunty, an Air Force pilot, returns home in uniform after her work. Kabir's grandfather, a retired BSF officer, now spends his time tending to the vegetable garden, running errands, and providing free Geography classes to neighbourhood kids. Anu's parents run a shop selling uniforms and garments. Her mother also contributes to a voluntary group that teaches knitting to women in the community. Anu's elder brother Rohan works as a software engineer in a company but volunteers on weekends to teach computer skills at a youth development programme.

This variety of activities helps us understand that work can take many forms — some done for earning money, some done out of love, care, or a sense of service. Each type of work has its own value in society.

notes.section_title: 13.1 Types of Activities: Economic and Non-Economic

content.text: Economic activities are those that involve money or are performed in exchange for money's worth for the parties involved. These activities generate income or wealth for the person performing them.

content.title: Examples of Economic Activities
items: A business person selling school bags in the market
A farmer selling produce in the market
A lawyer arguing a case and earning a fee
A truck driver transporting goods from one place to another
Workers employed in a car manufacturing factory
An Air Force pilot receiving a salary
A software engineer working for a company and getting a salary
A village post office employee paid a monthly salary
A teacher conducting online classes and charging a weekly fee
A farm labourer earning daily wages for tilling land

content.title: Key Terms in Economic Activities
content.text:
Salary: A fixed regular payment, generally paid monthly, by an employer to an employee.

Fee: A payment made to a person or organisation in exchange for professional advice or services (e.g., fee paid to a doctor or lawyer).

Wage: A payment made by the employer to the worker for a specific period of time, often calculated on a daily or hourly basis.

Payment in Kind: A non-cash payment received for work performed. For example, a farm labourer may receive part of their payment in the form of mangoes or other goods.

Non-economic activities are those that do not generate income or wealth but are done out of feelings like love, care, gratitude, respect, or a sense of duty. These activities do not involve monetary exchange but are valuable for personal relationships and social well-being.

content.title: Examples of Non-Economic Activities
items: Parents cooking food for the family
Mother helping her children with schoolwork
Youth taking care of their grandparents
Family members helping in the renovation of the house
A retired person providing free Geography classes to neighbourhood kids
Volunteering to teach computer skills at a youth development programme
Running errands for the home without payment

content.title: Understanding the Difference
content.text: The same type of work can be economic or non-economic depending on the context. For example, a teacher who teaches in a school for a salary is engaged in an economic activity, but the same teacher providing free tuition to underprivileged children after school hours is engaged in a non-economic activity. The intention and presence or absence of monetary exchange determine the classification.

notes.section_title: 13.2 Types of Economic Activities Based on Payment

content.text: Salaried employment refers to work where a person receives a fixed regular payment — usually monthly — from an employer. The person is typically employed on a long-term basis and receives benefits like job security, leave, and other entitlements depending on the nature of employment.

content.title: Examples of Salaried Employment in the Chapter
items: Geeta Aunty (Air Force pilot) receives a salary
Rohan (software engineer) receives a salary from his company
Kavya's aunt (village post office employee) is paid a monthly salary
Kavya's uncle (technician in construction company) receives a monthly salary

content.text: Wage employment involves payment made by an employer to a worker for a specific period of time. Wages are often calculated on a daily or hourly basis. This type of employment is common in agriculture, construction, and other manual labour sectors.

content.title: Example from the Chapter
content.text: Sahil, a farm labourer, works on a farmer's land using a tractor. He earns a daily wage for his efforts. This means he is paid for each day he works, rather than receiving a fixed monthly salary.

Payment in kind refers to non-cash payments received for work performed. Instead of receiving money, a worker may receive goods or services as compensation for their labour. This practice is still common in rural areas, particularly in agriculture.

content.title: Example from the Chapter
content.text: Sahil receives some payment for his work in cash, and the remaining part of his payment in the form of mangoes of equal value. The mangoes he receives as a part of his work are called payment in kind.

content.title: Key Point
content.text: Payment in kind has the same monetary value as cash payment — it is simply a different form of compensation. The total value received by the worker remains the same whether paid in cash, kind, or a combination of both.

Professionals such as doctors, lawyers, architects, and teachers often charge a fee for their services. A fee is a payment made in exchange for professional advice or services. Unlike salary which is regular and fixed, fees may be charged per service, per hour, or per session.

content.title: Example from the Chapter
content.text: Kavya's aunt, after completing her office work at the post office, conducts online school classes to help students prepare for exams. She charges a weekly fee for these classes. This is different from her salary at the post office.

Economic activities add value at each stage of the process of transforming something into another form. This is called value addition. When a person uses their skill, time, and effort to convert raw materials into finished goods, they increase the value of the product.

content.title: Example from the Chapter — Rajesh the Carpenter
content.text:
Rajesh, Kavya's father, is a carpenter. He buys wood from a nearby market for ₹600 to make furniture. He uses special tools and other materials to build the furniture. He then sells a chair for ₹1,000 in the market.

The wood for the chair costs ₹600. The remaining ₹400 (₹1,000 – ₹600) is the monetary value of Rajesh's skill, time, and effort that went into making that chair. By turning wood into furniture, Rajesh has added value to the wood. From buying the wood to selling the chair, these activities involve payment and are, therefore, part of economic activities.

content.title: Key Concept — Value Addition
content.text: Value addition is the process by which a person or business increases the worth of a product or service through their labour, skill, and effort. The value added is the difference between the cost of inputs (raw materials) and the price at which the finished product is sold.

content.title: Activities That Create Monetary Value
items: Baker — transforms flour, sugar, and other ingredients into bread, cakes, and pastries
Tailor — converts cloth into garments, adding value through stitching and design
Farmers repairing their tractor — maintaining equipment to continue productive work
Doctor — provides medical expertise to diagnose and treat illnesses
Scientist — conducts research that leads to new discoveries and innovations

content.title: Important Distinction
content.text: Not all activities that involve effort create monetary value. For example, parents cooking dinner for their family or a person taking care of a sick grandparent are valuable activities, but they do not generate monetary value. They are non-economic activities, even though they require time, skill, and effort.

While non-economic activities may not involve money, the value they generate is important in our lives. In India, the concept of sevā — selfless service — has deep cultural roots. Sevā is service performed without expecting anything in return, driven by compassion, duty, and a sense of responsibility toward others.

content.title: Examples of Sevā in Indian Traditions
items: Langars or community kitchens at gurudwaras serve food to every visitor for free, regardless of caste, creed, or religion
Temples distribute prasād to devotees as a form of blessing and community sharing
Churches and mosques organize community meals and support services for the needy

content.text: These practices foster a sense of satisfaction and gratitude for what we have. They are also a way of contributing to society without expecting anything in return.

The strength of community participation lies in the collective efforts of individuals working together for a common goal. When people come together voluntarily to improve their surroundings or contribute to society, they create value that cannot be measured in money.

The Swachh Bharat Abhiyan (Clean India Mission) is based on the collective efforts of all Indian citizens to keep our surroundings clean. Individually, we keep our homes and surroundings clean. People have also come together to clean up streets, roads, parks, and other public places or community areas. Together, these efforts lead to a clean home, neighbourhood, society, and nation.

Another example of collective community participation is the celebration of Van Mahotsav (the festival of forests) in India. This initiative aims to promote awareness about the value of trees and the conservation of forests. The initiative brings together members of the community for tree plantation drives, contributing to environmental protection and sustainability.

In India, during festivals, people gather to organize various activities. They decorate the place together and share the food that they cook. These activities are non-economic because they are not done for monetary gain. However, they hold great value as they strengthen community bonds, preserve cultural traditions, and create a sense of belonging and shared joy.

notes.section_title: Conclusion: The Value of All Work

topics.topic_name: Valuing Both Economic and Non-Economic Contributions

content.text: This chapter helps us understand that work has value beyond money. While economic activities generate income and add monetary value through the transformation of goods and services, non-economic activities contribute to social welfare, personal well-being, and the overall quality of life. Both types of activities are essential for a healthy, functioning society. The work done by parents at home, volunteers in the community, and individuals engaged in selfless service deserves recognition and respect alongside paid work. Understanding this helps us appreciate the dignity of all forms of work and the contributions of every person in our society.

glossary.definition: Activities that involve money or are performed in exchange for money's worth for the parties involved. These activities generate income or wealth.
Activities that do not generate income or wealth but are done out of feelings like love, care, gratitude, respect, or a sense of duty.
A fixed regular payment, generally paid monthly, by an employer to an employee.
A payment made by the employer to the worker for a specific period of time, often calculated on a daily or hourly basis.
A payment made to a person or organisation in exchange for professional advice or services.
A non-cash payment received for work performed, such as goods or services instead of money.
The process by which a person or business increases the worth of a product or service through their labour, skill, and effort. It is the difference between the cost of inputs and the selling price.
A place where people engage in the exchange of goods and services. People may exchange goods and services for other goods, but in most markets, these are exchanged for money.
Selfless service performed without expecting anything in return, driven by compassion, duty, and a sense of responsibility toward others.
A nationwide campaign launched by the Government of India to promote cleanliness and hygiene, based on the collective efforts of citizens.
The 'festival of forests' celebrated in India to promote awareness about the value of trees and the conservation of forests through community tree plantation drives.
A community kitchen in Sikh gurudwaras that serves free meals to all visitors, regardless of caste, creed, or religion.
Food offered to a deity in Hindu temples and then distributed to devotees as a form of blessing and community sharing.

summary_points: Work can be classified into economic activities (involving money or money's worth) and non-economic activities (done out of love, care, or service without monetary gain).

Economic activities include various forms of compensation: salary (fixed monthly payment), wage (payment for specific time period), fee (payment for professional services), and payment in kind (non-cash payment like goods).

Value addition is the process where economic activities increase the worth of a product through labour, skill, and effort — for example, a carpenter turning wood worth ₹600 into a chair sold for ₹1,000 adds ₹400 of value.

Non-economic activities, though they don't generate money, are extremely valuable — they include sevā (selfless service) like langars in gurudwaras, community participation in Swachh Bharat Abhiyan and Van Mahotsav, and festive gatherings that strengthen social bonds.
Both economic and non-economic activities are essential for society — one generates income and economic growth, while the other fosters social welfare, community strength, and personal well-being.
